topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee shop air felt suddenly thin, stinging my throat. Liam was laughing, a bright, easy sound that used to instantly pull me into his orbit, but now it just bounced off the wall I was trying to build around myself. He was talking animatedly about a shared memory from elementary school, a memory I distinctly recalled being one of our defining moments. When he paused, looking around vaguely, he didn't look at me. He just shrugged, a casual dismissal that felt like a physical shove. My jaw tightened, the muscles in my neck corded with a sudden, sharp tension. I watched him, cataloging the blankness in his eyes, a cold knot forming deep in my stomach. I took a slow sip of my lukewarm latte, forcing my expression into something neutral, something impenetrable. "Right," I managed, the word sounding brittle, like old glass.

[story 2]

I stood by the lockers, the fluorescent lights humming a grating soundtrack to my rising frustration. Sarah approached, her smile warm and familiar, and I braced myself for the usual easy banter. Instead, she started recounting a story about a summer trip, and when she reached the part where she mentioned our secret pact by the creek, she faltered. She blinked, a flicker of confusion crossing her face, as if trying to pull a thread from a frayed tapestry. The silence that followed was heavy, suffocating. I felt a flush creep up my neck, hot and undeniable. I clenched my fists inside my pockets, my knuckles turning white. I forced a laugh that sounded unnaturally high-pitched, a sound that didn't reach my eyes. "Oh, that sounds lovely," I said, my voice carefully modulated, trying to keep the tremor out of it. I needed to walk away before I did something regrettable.

[story 3]

It was the way he looked at me that truly cut. Across the crowded party, Liam caught my eye, and for a split second, I saw something flicker—a recognition, maybe, followed immediately by a blank wall. He offered a quick, perfunctory nod, the kind one gives a stranger they’ve briefly encountered. The atmosphere around him shifted; the easy camaraderie we once shared evaporated, leaving a strange, hollow space where warmth used to reside. I felt a sudden, intense urge to pull my arm away from the group, to physically detach myself from the proximity of his presence. My breathing hitched, shallow and quick. I focused intently on a distant point on the ceiling, trying to anchor myself against the sudden, destabilizing feeling of being utterly unseen.
